title: Deep scratches remain after 80 grit
problem: 80 grit removed material but left deep scratches.
likely_cause: 80 grit is a heavy removal grit and leaves a scratch pattern that must be refined in steps.
recommended_grit: 120, then 180, then 220 or 320 as needed.
wet_or_dry: Dry for early removal and prep.
steps: Sand evenly with 120 grit until the 80-grit marks are lighter.
avoid: Do not jump from 80 directly to 320 or finer.
success_check: The surface shows one even finer scratch pattern with no remaining 80-grit lines.

title: 320 grit does not remove 180 grit scratches
problem: Scratches from 180 grit remain after sanding with 320 grit.
likely_cause: The jump from 180 to 320 is too large for the depth of the scratches.
recommended_grit: 220 or 240 before returning to 320.
wet_or_dry: Dry for normal prep work.
steps: Clean the dust before switching grits.
Use 320 only after the deeper lines are gone.
avoid: Do not press harder with 320 grit to force removal.
success_check: No visible 180-grit lines remain before 320 grit.

title: Coarse scratches show on wood
problem: Wood still shows visible scratch lines after prep sanding.
likely_cause: The previous grit pattern was not fully removed before moving finer.
recommended_grit: 120 to remove heavy marks, then 180 and 220 for prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Sand with the grain when possible.
Use the grit that still removes the visible lines.
Remove dust and inspect under good light.
avoid: Do not skip directly to fine finishing grits on rough wood.
success_check: The wood feels even and has no isolated deep scratch lines.

title: Scratches remain on metal after prep
problem: Metal shows straight or uneven sanding scratches.
likely_cause: The metal was not refined through enough intermediate grits.
recommended_grit: 180, 220, 320, then 400 or 600 if a finer prep is needed.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet can help at finer stages where suitable.
steps: Sand with the next finer grit until the old pattern is uniform.
Change direction slightly between grits to see progress.
avoid: Do not leave loose grit or debris on the surface.
success_check: The old scratch direction is no longer visible.

title: Deep lines remain on clear coat
problem: Fine sanding leaves some deeper lines in clear coat.
likely_cause: A previous grit or debris created scratches deeper than the current fine grit can remove.
recommended_grit: Return one step coarser only as needed, then progress 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Use clean water and light pressure.
Refine only the affected area evenly.
Move finer once the deeper lines are gone.
avoid: Do not dry sand a fine clear coat finish unless the coating instructions call for it.
success_check: The haze is uniform with no isolated deep lines.

title: Paint clogs the sheet quickly
problem: Paint residue loads into the abrasive and sanding slows.
likely_cause: Soft paint or coating residue is smearing under pressure.
recommended_grit: Use the grit needed for the stage; often 120 to 220 for removal or prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for removal; wet only for suitable fine finishing stages.
avoid: Do not keep sanding with a glazed or loaded sheet.
success_check: The paper cuts instead of smearing paint residue.

title: Primer dust loads the paper
problem: Primer dust packs into the abrasive during prep.
likely_cause: Dust is not being cleared often enough or the sheet is worn.
recommended_grit: 320 to 600 depending on the coating stage.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on coating instructions and finish stage.
steps: Let the primer cure as directed by the coating maker.
avoid: Do not sand uncured coating.
success_check: The surface powders cleanly and the sheet keeps cutting.

title: Wood dust clogs the paper
problem: Wood dust fills the abrasive and the sheet feels smooth.
likely_cause: Dust buildup, excess pressure, or a worn sheet is reducing cutting.
recommended_grit: Use the correct prep grit, commonly 120 to 220 for wood smoothing.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Brush or wipe dust away often.
Use a sanding block on flat areas.
avoid: Do not use a clogged sheet for final prep.
success_check: Fresh abrasive texture remains visible and the surface smooths evenly.

title: Finish smears while sanding
problem: The finish gums up instead of sanding to powder.
likely_cause: The finish may be too soft for sanding or too much heat is being created.
recommended_grit: Use fine prep grits such as 320 to 600 when the finish is ready.
wet_or_dry: Light dry sanding or wet sanding where the finish and stage allow.
steps: Allow more cure time if the coating is soft.
Use fresh paper if loading continues.
avoid: Do not grind soft finish with heavy pressure.
success_check: The finish sands evenly instead of smearing.

title: Wet sanding slurry builds up
problem: Wet sanding residue collects and makes cutting inconsistent.
likely_cause: The surface and sheet are not being rinsed often enough.
recommended_grit: Use the planned fine grit sequence, such as 1000 to 3000.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Keep clean water on the surface.
avoid: Do not let dirty slurry dry on the work area.
success_check: The wet sanding haze stays even and controlled.

title: Wood still rough after 120 grit
problem: Wood fibers or roughness remain after sanding with 120 grit.
likely_cause: 120 grit may still be an early prep step and needs follow-up refinement.
recommended_grit: 150 or 180, then 220 for smoother prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
avoid: Do not jump from rough 120 grit directly to very fine grit.
success_check: The wood feels consistently smooth before finishing.

title: Wood still rough after 220 grit
problem: The wood still feels raised or uneven after 220 grit.
likely_cause: The roughness was not fully removed at the earlier grit or the grain was raised.
recommended_grit: Return to 180 if roughness remains, then finish with 220.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Identify whether roughness is across the whole surface or only spots.
avoid: Do not polish rough wood with 320 before it is leveled.
success_check: The surface feels even with no rough islands.

title: Metal still feels rough
problem: Metal still has roughness after sanding.
likely_cause: Burrs, oxidation, or deeper scratches need a grit that still cuts them.
recommended_grit: Start around 120 or 180 for roughness, then 220 and 320.
wet_or_dry: Dry for initial prep; wet for finer stages where suitable.
steps: Sand with a grit that cuts the roughness.
avoid: Do not start too fine on rough metal.
success_check: The metal feels even and the scratch pattern is consistent.

title: Plastic still feels rough
problem: Plastic remains rough or uneven after sanding.
likely_cause: The surface needs gentler step-by-step refinement to avoid deep scratches.
recommended_grit: 320 or 400 for prep, then 600+ for finer finish needs.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine stages when suitable.
steps: Rinse residue before each finer grit.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure that can gouge or heat the plastic.
success_check: The surface feels even with a controlled fine scratch pattern.

title: Rough spots remain after fine grit
problem: Most of the surface is smooth but some spots remain rough.
likely_cause: The rough spots were not leveled before moving to a finer grit.
recommended_grit: Return to the last grit that can remove the roughness.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the project stage.
steps: Mark the rough spots visually if needed.
Sand those areas evenly with the working grit.
Move finer only after the spots are gone.
avoid: Do not overwork one tiny spot and create a low area.
success_check: The surface feels consistent from edge to edge.

title: Normal haze after wet sanding
problem: Wet sanding leaves the surface dull or cloudy.
likely_cause: Fine wet sanding normally creates a uniform haze before polishing or final refinement.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Polish after the final grit when gloss is required.
avoid: Do not expect sanding alone to restore gloss.
success_check: The haze is even and free of deeper scratches.

title: Clear coat haze after 1000 grit
problem: 1000 grit wet sanding leaves a dull clear coat.
likely_cause: 1000 grit is often an intermediate leveling step, not the final gloss stage.
recommended_grit: 1500, then 2000 and 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Polish when the sanding haze is fine and uniform.
avoid: Do not stop at 1000 grit when a gloss finish is expected.
success_check: The haze becomes finer and more uniform after each grit.

title: Plastic haze after wet sanding
problem: Plastic looks cloudy after fine wet sanding.
likely_cause: The surface has been refined but still needs finer grits or polishing if gloss is desired.
recommended_grit: 1500, 2000, and 3000 for polishing preparation.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
avoid: Do not create heat with heavy pressure.
success_check: The haze is fine, even, and ready for the next finish step.

title: Wet sanding haze is uneven
problem: Some areas are dull while others are still glossy or scratched.
likely_cause: Pressure, water, or sanding coverage was inconsistent.
recommended_grit: Repeat the current fine grit before moving finer.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Move finer only when the haze is even.
avoid: Do not polish an unevenly sanded surface and expect uniform results.
success_check: The entire area has a consistent fine haze.

title: Skipped from 120 to 320
problem: 320 grit does not remove the scratches left by 120 grit.
likely_cause: The grit progression skipped too many intermediate steps.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 before 320.
wet_or_dry: Dry for prep.
steps: Sand until the 120-grit lines are gone.
avoid: Do not use fine grit as a substitute for proper progression.
success_check: Each grit removes the previous grit pattern before moving on.

title: Choosing a starting grit
problem: It is unclear which grit to start with.
likely_cause: The task stage has not been identified yet.
recommended_grit: 60-120 for removal, 150-240 for prep, 280-400 for finish prep, 600+ for fine stages.
wet_or_dry: Dry for removal and prep; wet for fine finishing where suitable.
steps: Identify whether you are removing, smoothing, finishing, or polishing prep.
Start with the least aggressive grit that works.
avoid: Do not start with the coarsest grit unless heavy removal is needed.
success_check: The grit removes the defect without creating unnecessary deep scratches.

title: Using the 80-3000 assorted kit
problem: The kit has many grits and the right order is unclear.
likely_cause: Each grit range serves a different stage.
recommended_grit: Use 80-120 for removal, 180-400 for prep, 600-3000 for fine finishing and polishing prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for lower grits; wet for fine stages where suitable.
steps: Choose the grit range for the task stage.
Do not use every grit if the surface does not need it.
Avoid large jumps when scratches matter.
Use high grits for fine refinement.
avoid: Do not use 1000-3000 for heavy removal.
success_check: Each grit has a clear purpose in the sequence.

title: What grit for wood prep
problem: The wood needs to be prepared but the grit is unclear.
likely_cause: Wood prep depends on how rough the surface is before sanding.
recommended_grit: 120 or 150 for rough prep, then 180 or 220 for smoother prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Start only as coarse as needed.
Sand evenly with the grain when possible.
avoid: Do not over-sand soft areas with heavy pressure.
success_check: The wood feels smooth and shows an even scratch pattern.

title: What grit for paint prep
problem: Paint needs prep sanding before another coat.
likely_cause: Paint prep needs enough tooth without deep scratches.
recommended_grit: 220 to 400 depending on the stage and coating instructions.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on coating stage.
steps: Let the coating cure as directed.
Sand only enough to smooth and prepare.
avoid: Do not use heavy-removal grit between finish coats.
success_check: The surface is smooth, clean, and not cut through.

title: Grit is too coarse between coats
problem: Sanding between coats leaves scratches or cuts through coating.
likely_cause: The grit is intended for removal instead of light intercoat prep.
recommended_grit: 320 to 600 depending on coating stage.
wet_or_dry: Light dry sanding or wet sanding where suitable.
steps: Confirm the coating is ready to sand.
avoid: Do not use 80, 120, or 180 grit for fine intercoat sanding.
success_check: The coating is lightly dulled and smooth without deep scratches.

title: Sanding cuts through between coats
problem: Sanding removes too much coating in some areas.
likely_cause: Pressure is too high, grit is too coarse, or edges are being overworked.
recommended_grit: Use a finer grit such as 400 or 600 for delicate intercoat smoothing.
wet_or_dry: Light dry sanding or wet sanding if appropriate.
steps: Clean before deciding on the next coat.
avoid: Do not try to level thick defects with fine intercoat sanding.
success_check: The surface is smooth without exposing lower layers.

title: Primer scratches show through
problem: Scratches remain visible after coating over primer.
likely_cause: The primer was not refined enough before the next coat.
recommended_grit: 320 or 400 before recoating, depending on coating instructions.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet as suitable for the primer stage.
steps: Recoat only after the surface is uniform.
avoid: Do not coat over isolated deep sanding marks.
success_check: The primer has a uniform dull surface before coating.

title: Swirls remain after 1000 grit
problem: Curved or circular marks remain after fine sanding.
likely_cause: 1000 grit marks may need further refinement or the pattern was inconsistent.
recommended_grit: 1500, 2000, then 3000 as needed.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Continue finer until marks are uniform.
avoid: Do not press harder with the same grit to chase swirls.
success_check: Circular marks are replaced by a uniform fine haze.

title: Swirls from dirty wet sanding water
problem: Fine sanding creates random curved scratches.
likely_cause: Loose grit or debris in the water is scratching the surface.
recommended_grit: Repeat the current fine grit, then move finer.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Wipe before moving to the next grit.
avoid: Do not keep sanding with contaminated slurry.
success_check: New scratches stop appearing and the haze is even.

title: Random swirl pattern remains
problem: A visible pattern remains after sanding.
likely_cause: The final grit was not fine enough or the previous scratches were not fully removed.
recommended_grit: Move gradually through finer grits up to the finish goal.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine finish stages.
steps: Return one grit step if needed.
Progress finer only after the pattern is even.
avoid: Do not skip refinement steps when the finish will be inspected closely.
success_check: The pattern is fine, uniform, and ready for polishing or finishing.

title: Paper tears on sharp edges
problem: The sheet catches and rips on an edge or corner.
likely_cause: Sharp edges cut into the backing under pressure.
recommended_grit: Use the correct task grit, but reduce pressure near edges.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the task.
steps: Sand off the edge carefully instead of dragging into it.
Use a block where it helps control pressure.
avoid: Do not pull the sheet hard across sharp corners.
success_check: The sheet glides without catching.

title: Paper tears at the fold
problem: The sheet splits where it was folded.
likely_cause: A hard crease concentrates stress in one line.
recommended_grit: Any grit can tear if sharply creased.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the task.
steps: Fold gently without crushing the backing.
Use a smaller piece if needed.
Move the fold location as the abrasive wears.
Use a block on flat areas.
avoid: Do not create a tight crease and pull hard under pressure.
success_check: The sheet stays intact through normal strokes.

title: Paper tears from heavy pressure
problem: The sheet wears through or tears during sanding.
likely_cause: Excess pressure creates heat and stress on the backing.
recommended_grit: Use a grit coarse enough to cut without forcing it.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the task.
steps: Let the abrasive do the cutting.
Switch to a coarser grit if removal is too slow.
avoid: Do not use pressure to make a too-fine grit remove material.
success_check: Cutting improves without tearing the sheet.

title: Paint removal is too slow
problem: Paint is not coming off efficiently.
likely_cause: The grit may be too fine, loaded, or worn for removal.
recommended_grit: 60, 80, or 120 for removal depending on the surface and finish goal.
wet_or_dry: Dry for removal.
steps: Use a coarser grit suited to removal.
Clear dust and paint residue often.
Refine scratches with finer grits afterward.
avoid: Do not remove paint with 400+ grit unless only light scuffing is needed.
success_check: Paint removal speeds up without excessive pressure.

title: Fine grit used for removal
problem: Sanding takes a long time and little material is removed.
likely_cause: Fine grit refines scratches but is not efficient for heavy removal.
recommended_grit: 60 to 120 for heavy removal, then step finer.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Switch to a grit that actually cuts the defect.
Finish with finer grits only after leveling.
avoid: Do not keep rubbing with a fine grit when the defect is still high or deep.
success_check: The defect reduces visibly in a reasonable number of strokes.

title: Worn sheet is not cutting
problem: The sheet feels smooth and sanding no longer changes the surface.
likely_cause: The abrasive is worn or loaded.
recommended_grit: Stay with the selected grit but use a fresh sheet.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the task.
steps: Replace the sheet if worn smooth.
avoid: Do not compensate for a worn sheet with heavy pressure.
success_check: A fresh sheet cuts noticeably better.

title: Wood prep before finish
problem: Wood needs a smooth, consistent surface before finish.
likely_cause: The prep sequence has not reached an even final scratch pattern.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Start with the least aggressive grit that removes roughness.
Finish prep at 180 or 220 based on the finish goal.
avoid: Do not leave coarse scratch marks under finish.
success_check: The wood feels smooth and looks even before finish is applied.

title: Metal prep before paint
problem: Metal needs sanding before coating.
likely_cause: The surface needs cleaning and a consistent prep pattern.
recommended_grit: 180 to 320 depending on existing roughness and coating instructions.
wet_or_dry: Dry for prep; wet only where suitable for finer steps.
steps: Remove roughness or oxidation with the needed grit.
avoid: Do not coat over loose debris or deep random scratches.
success_check: The metal is clean and uniformly prepared.

title: Plastic prep before paint
problem: Plastic needs light sanding before coating.
likely_cause: Plastic can scratch or heat easily if the grit or pressure is too aggressive.
recommended_grit: 320 to 600 depending on coating instructions and surface condition.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the coating stage.
steps: Create a uniform fine scratch pattern.
avoid: Do not gouge plastic with coarse grit unless heavy shaping is intentional.
success_check: The surface is evenly dulled without deep scratches.

title: Clear coat leveling before polish
problem: Clear coat needs fine leveling before polishing.
likely_cause: The surface has texture that requires controlled fine wet sanding.
recommended_grit: 1000 or 1500 to start only when appropriate, then 2000 and 3000.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Confirm the coating is ready for sanding.
Use clean water and light pressure.
avoid: Do not sand through edges or raised body lines.
success_check: Texture is leveled and the haze is uniform before polishing.

title: Using the assorted kit for heavy removal
problem: The task needs fast material removal.
likely_cause: Starting too fine makes sanding slow.
recommended_grit: 80 or 120, then refine with 180, 220, and 320 as needed.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Start with 80 or 120 only if heavy removal is needed.
Stop once the defect is reduced.
Refine the coarse scratches with intermediate grits.
Finish at the grit needed for the next step.
avoid: Do not leave 80-grit scratches as the final surface.
success_check: Material is removed efficiently and then refined.

title: Using the assorted kit for finish prep
problem: The surface needs fine prep before coating or finishing.
likely_cause: Finish prep needs finer grits than heavy removal.
recommended_grit: 220, 320, 400, or 600 depending on finish stage.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on coating and stage.
steps: Use 220 or 320 to refine prep scratches.
Use 400 or 600 for lighter fine prep where appropriate.
Check that no coarse scratches remain.
avoid: Do not use 80 or 120 for fine finish prep.
success_check: The surface is smooth, clean, and evenly prepared.

title: Using the assorted kit for polishing prep
problem: A glossy surface needs fine sanding before polish.
likely_cause: The surface must be refined through high grits before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Start only as coarse as the defect requires.
Polish after the final fine grit if gloss is required.
avoid: Do not use low grits for polishing prep.
success_check: The final haze is very fine and uniform.

title: Uneven finish from pressure
problem: The sanded surface looks patchy or uneven.
likely_cause: Pressure or stroke coverage was inconsistent.
recommended_grit: Repeat the current stage grit before moving finer.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the stage.
steps: Use a block on flat surfaces.
Clean and inspect before changing grits.
avoid: Do not focus pressure in one small area.
success_check: The scratch pattern and sheen are uniform.

title: Finger grooves from hand sanding
problem: The surface has uneven grooves from fingers.
likely_cause: Finger pressure concentrated force in narrow lines.
recommended_grit: Use the current prep grit, then refine normally.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on stage.
steps: Use a sanding block on flat surfaces.
Move finer only when the surface is even.
avoid: Do not press with fingertips on flat finish areas.
success_check: The surface no longer shows narrow pressure tracks.

title: Patchy surface after grit change
problem: The surface looks uneven after switching grits.
likely_cause: The previous grit pattern was not fully removed across the whole surface.
recommended_grit: Return to the previous grit or current grit until uniform.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on stage.
steps: Sand until the older pattern is gone.
avoid: Do not move finer while old scratches remain in spots.
success_check: The whole surface changes to the same finer pattern.

title: Dust creates extra scratches
problem: Random scratches appear while dry sanding.
likely_cause: Loose grit or debris is trapped under the paper.
recommended_grit: Repeat the current grit after cleaning, then move finer.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
avoid: Do not sand over loose debris.
success_check: Random new scratches stop appearing.
